elements.sent-2411-001: Existing approaches to drone visual geo-localization predominantly adopt the image-based setting, where a single drone-view snapshot is matched with images from other platforms.
elements.sent-2411-002: Such task formulation, however, underutilizes the inherent video output of the drone and is sensitive to occlusions and environmental constraints.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address these limitations, we formulate a new video-based drone geo-localization task and propose the Video2BEV paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paradigm transforms the video into a Bird's Eye View (BEV), simplifying the subsequent matching process.
elements.sent-2411-005: In particular, we employ Gaussian Splatting to reconstruct a 3D scene and obtain the BEV projection.
elements.sent-2411-006: Different from the existing transform methods, \eg, polar transform, our BEVs preserve more fine-grained details without significant distortion.
elements.sent-2411-007: To further improve model scalability toward diverse BEVs and satellite figures, our Video2BEV paradigm also incorporates a diffusion-based module for generating hard negative samples, which facilitates discriminative feature learning.
elements.sent-2411-008: To validate our approach, we introduce UniV, a new video-based geo-localization dataset that extends the image-based University-1652 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-009: UniV features flight paths at $30^\circ$ and $45^\circ$ elevation angles with increased frame rates of up to 10 frames per second (FPS).
elements.sent-2411-010: Extensive experiments on the UniV dataset show that our Video2BEV paradigm achieves competitive recall rates and outperforms conventional video-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-011: Compared to other methods, our proposed approach exhibits robustness at lower elevations with more occlusions.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/fig1_video_better_img.pdf} \caption{ Typical failure cases for image-based drone geo-localization. { For image queries, the \textcolor{red}{core areas} in ground-truth are occluded by another building, largely compromising the spatial matching. In contrast, video queries usually contain unoccluded frames in a circling flight, and thus could reflect a more comprehensive view of the target location. Drone video inputs are relatively robust to occlusions. } } \label{fig:vieo_better_img}
elements.sent-2411-012: Given the visual information captured by the drone, drone visual geo-localization aims to retrieve the image of the same location from another platform, \eg, satellite, which is typically associated with the off-line GPS metadata~\cite{zheng_1652_mm20 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This capability enables drones to locate themselves even in GPS-unavailable areas, such as those between tall buildings or in rural regions.
elements.sent-2411-014: The common setting is based on the snapshot image from the drone as a query to search the matched location in the satellite-view candidate pool.
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite its potential, image-based drone geo-localization faces three primary challenges: (1) The single image from the drone view is often affected by occlusion and other environmental constraints.
elements.sent-2411-016: This paper attempts to address three challenges in drone visual geo-localization.
elements.sent-2411-017: First, existing approaches~\cite{zheng_1652_mm20,wang_dwdr_arxiv22,wang_lpn_tcsvt21,deuser_sample4geo_iccv23 predominantly adopt the image-based setting, where a single drone-view snapshot is matched with images from other platforms.}
elements.sent-2411-018: As shown in~\reffig{fig:vieo_better_img , such task formulation underutilizes the inherent video output of the drone and is sensitive to occlusions and environmental constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-019: To mitigate this, we formulate a new video-based drone geo-localization task, which harnesses the drone video as input to capture the target location comprehensively.
elements.sent-2411-020: By controlling the drone to achieve unobstructed viewpoints, the video input significantly reduces the impact of occasional occlusions present in the single frame, thereby improving the performance on all our re-implemented methods (see ~\reffig{fig:video_better_img_performance ).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/fig1_5_result_img_vid.pdf} \caption{ Performance comparisons of leveraging image data (Image2Image) or video data (Video2Image or Image2Video) with methods including LPN~\cite{wang_lpn_tcsvt21}, FSRA~\cite{dai_fsra_tcsvt21}, DWDR~\cite{wang_dwdr_arxiv22}, and Sample4Geo~\cite{deuser_sample4geo_iccv23}. We report the Average Precision (AP) metric. For a fair comparison, we keep the same number of data in the gallery. We could observe that all our re-implemented methods arrive at better performance when adopting video query or gallery. \label{fig:video_better_img_performance} }
elements.sent-2411-021: Second, geometric transformations based on a single image often introduce edge distortion and blurring (see~\reffig{fig:comparison_alignment ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: Existing approaches~\cite{shi_safa_nips19,wang_hcnet_nips24 usually apply the transformation pre-processing to align the input images collected from different platforms.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Such transformations coarsely align the images, thereby easing spatial comparison for deeply-learned models.
elements.sent-2411-024: For instance, Shi~\etal~\cite{shi_safa_nips19 propose transforming satellite views to ground views via the polar transformation, which generally aligns the layout of the transformed images with fisheye ground-view photos.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Similarly, Wang~\etal~\cite{wang_hcnet_nips24 manipulate panoramic ground views to satellite views via the spherical transformation, simplifying the comparison with satellite-view data.}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, both the pre-processing transformation methods suffer from spatial distortion and blurring, leading to incomplete image-level alignment.
elements.sent-2411-027: Since the input of our task is the video with different viewpoints along the flight, we propose a Video2BEV paradigm that transforms the video into a Bird's Eye View (BEV).
elements.sent-2411-028: Specifically, we employ Gaussian Splatting~\cite{kerbl_3dgs_tg23 to reconstruct a 3D scene and obtain the BEV projection.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Unlike existing transformation methods, such as polar and spherical transformations, our transformation is not based on a single 2D image but a 3D scene derived from videos.
elements.sent-2411-030: Therefore, the core area of our BEVs preserve more fine-grained textures without significant distortion or blurring.
elements.sent-2411-031: Third, many locations share similar patterns, such as architectural styles, making it challenging to distinguish between them using a single image alone.
elements.sent-2411-032: To address this, we resort to the drone video input in this work and conduct more discriminative feature learning.
elements.sent-2411-033: To enhance model scalability and discriminative capability, our Video2BEV paradigm incorporates a diffusion-based module for generating hard negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-034: This module generates appearance-similar BEVs, motivating the model to focus on fine-grained details to discriminate diffusion-generated BEV hard negatives and BEVs derived from videos.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/fig2_compare.pdf} \caption{ Prevailing image-based geometric transformation (a) Satellite to Ground transformation by the polar transformation~\cite{shi_safa_nips19}, (b) Ground to Satellite transformation by the spherical transformation~\cite{wang_hcnet_nips24}. Our Drone video to Satellite transformation is shown in (c). Compared to image-based approaches, our method, fully leveraging the comprehensive view from the free-of-lunch drone videos, mitigates severe distortion and blurring. } \label{fig:comparison_alignment}
elements.sent-2411-035: In an attempt to overcome the aforementioned challenges, this paper {(1) adopts the video setting, (2) transforms video to BEVs derived from reconstructed 3D scenes, (3) introduces a diffusion model to generate hard negatives.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Given the lack of a video-based drone geo-localization benchmark, we introduce UniV, a new video-based geo-localization dataset that extends the image-based University-1652 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-037: UniV features flight paths at two different elevation angles with increased frame rates up to 10 frames per second (FPS).
elements.sent-2411-038: Compared to other methods, our proposed approach exhibits greater robustness at lower elevations with more occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-039: To summarize, the key contributions of our work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-040: We present a Video2BEV paradigm that transforms drone-view videos to Bird's Eye Views (BEVs), easing subsequent matching with satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-041: In particular, our Video2BEV introduces 3D Gaussian Splatting for geometric projection, while also including a hard negative generation module to learn from diverse BEVs and satellite images.
elements.sent-2411-042: Given the lack of a video-based drone geo-localization benchmark, we introduce a new video-based geolocalization dataset, UniV, with two elevation angles and 10 FPS.
elements.sent-2411-043: This dataset contains drone videos, satellite images, and ground images, and is closer to real-world scenarios containing typical cases, \eg, occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-044: Extensive experiments on the proposed UniV dataset show: (1) Image-based queries are highly sensitive to occlusions and environmental changes, such as occlusions, whereas video-based queries exhibit greater robustness (see~\reffig{fig:video_better_img_performance ).}
elements.sent-2411-045: We observe a similar result on the drone video with the lower elevation angle, which exposes more occlusions.

elements.sent-2411-046: Visual geo-localization is to locate the position via the visual information, which is usually formulated as a sub-task of image retrieval~\cite{zheng_1652_mm20 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The ancient people could check the surroundings to find their location via the paper map.
elements.sent-2411-048: Similarly, visual geo-localization searches the relevant position candidates recording in other platforms, \eg, satellite.
elements.sent-2411-049: The primary challenge of this task is due to the inherent difference among multiple platforms, \ie, appearance changes due to various viewpoints~\cite{shi_safa_nips19 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Therefore, previous methods focus on alignment to establish spatial correspondence between these platforms, which can be coarsely divided into two families, \ie, image-level alignment and feature-level alignment.
elements.sent-2411-051: For image-level alignment, Shi~\etal~\cite{shi_safa_nips19 first propose to leverage polar transformation to warp satellite images to the ground view.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Other methods~\cite{wang_hcnet_nips24,li_unsupervised_geo_cvpr24 transform panoramic ground images to the satellite view by proposed spherical transformation.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Regmi~\etal~\cite{regmi_gan_geo_iccv19 utilize generative adversarial networks to synthesize aerial images for matching with ground images.}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, the transformed images often suffer from severe distortion, blurring, and unrealistic content, which hinders fine-grained alignment between the two distinctive viewpoints.
elements.sent-2411-055: For implicit feature-level alignment, prior works~\cite{zheng_1652_mm20,deuser_sample4geo_iccv23,zhu_transgeo_cvpr22,sun_geocapsnet_icme19,zhang_geodtr_aaai23,zhang_geodtr_pami24,mi_congeo_eccv24 tend to rely on the power of neural networks or loss functions to align features between the query and gallery.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Other methods focus on the explicit alignment of features from different views.
elements.sent-2411-057: Among these, some methods~\cite{liu_cvact_cvpr19,shi_orientation_cvpr20,shi_cvft_aaai20 encode orientation information and utilize it for feature-level alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Some methods propose to establish key-point~\cite{lin_joint_tip22,song_unified_tip24 or region alignment~\cite{dai_fsra_tcsvt21,zeng_graphgeo_tmm22,wu_camp_tgrs24,wang_mfrgn_mm24 in the feature level.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Previous works overlook the viewpoint variation within drone-view videos, treating them in an image-based setting.
elements.sent-2411-060: In this work, we aim to unlock the potential of videos captured by a drone and mitigate view disparity via the transformation without obvious distortion or blurring.. Video Understanding.
elements.sent-2411-061: The early works, \eg, Carreira~\etal~\cite{carreira_i3d_cvpr17 and Shen~\etal~\cite{shen_stepnet_tmm24 , leverage the two-stream 3D convolution network that combines video data with corresponding optical flow.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: In this way, motion information is adopted as the guidance for the fusion of different frames.
elements.sent-2411-063: Instead of adopting the 3D convolution, Feichtenhofer~\etal~\cite{feichtenhofer_slowfast_cvpr19 propose to deploy a two-branch 2D convolution network to fuse spatial semantic information and motion information together.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Recently, attention mechanisms have shown their priority across many tasks~\cite{arnab_vivit_iccv21,vaswani_attention_nips17,liu_swin_iccv21,darcet_register_arxiv23 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: However, the computational cost of processing video data is substantial~\cite{han_vit_survey_pami22 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Many methods focus on designing efficient attention operations and training strategies.
elements.sent-2411-067: For efficient attention operations, Liu~\etal~\cite{liu_videoswin_cvpr22 propose a 3D shifted window mechanism based on multi-head attention to fuse inherent complementary information of videos.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Son~\etal~\cite{son_csta_cvpr24 propose CNN-based attention, modeling the ordering reliance in video frames and capturing the long-term dependency.}
elements.sent-2411-069: For effective training strategies, Tong~\etal~\cite{tong_videomae_nips22 introduce mask image modeling~\cite{he_mae_cvpr22 into video data in a self-supervised manner, leveraging complementary information across different frames.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: To keep task-specific and shared knowledge across different tasks, Peirone~\etal~\cite{peirone_backpack_cvpr24 treat video understanding as a unified task and propose to train task-specific heads and a cross-task backbone simultaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Different from the daily videos, drone videos for geo-localization typically contain multi-view information for the target location~\cite{wang_environment_pr24,mildenhall_nerf_eccv20 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, rather than adopting the off-the-shelf video backbone, we propose a Video2BEV transformation to leverage the 3D geometric correspondences and enable a straightforward spatial alignment for matching.
elements.sent-2411-073: Given the lack of a video-based drone geo-localization benchmark, we collect a new dataset dubbed UniV involving the video modality.
elements.sent-2411-074: We follow the building information and the protocol of the existing University-1652 dataset~\cite{zheng_1652_mm20 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: The UniV dataset encompasses 1,652 buildings in 72 universities from three platforms, \ie, ground, satellite, and drone cameras.

elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/fig3_architecture.pdf} \caption{ {The overview of the Video2BEV paradigm. \textbf{Video2BEV Transformation (left).}} Given drone-view video {$V_{drn}$} containing multi-view frames, we adopt 3D Gaussian Splatting~(3DGS) to reconstruct the scene at first. Then we render the scene from a Bird-Eye-View to get the {projection} (BEVs). {Considering the region of the core area, we further crop BEVs for training. We can observe that} BEVs exhibit resemblances to the corresponding satellite-view images. \textbf{Hard Negative Sample Synthesis (right).} Given captions generated by an {off-the-shelf} visual-LLM~\cite{hu_minicpm_arxiv24}, we fine-tune a stable-diffusion model~\cite{rombach_stable_diffusion_cvpr22} with LoRA~\cite{hu_lora_iclr22}, and conduct inference to synthesize samples which serve as negative samples for subsequent usage. \textbf{Model Architecture (middle).} Given outputs of the proposed Video2BEV transformation, we extract embeddings by a shared encoder for satellite images $x_{sat}$ and BEVs $x_{bev}$, supervised by the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L}_{C}$ and the instance loss $\mathcal{L}_{I}$. Then we extract embeddings from synthetic BEVs $x_{s\text{-}bev}$ and adopt {Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs)} to fuse both positive and negative samples, supervised by the matching loss $\mathcal{L}_M$. Similar operations are also applied for satellite-view images which are omitted. } \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-103: During the flight around the target building, the viewpoints of the camera vary, resulting in captured drone-view videos that contain rich multi-view information about both the target building and surrounding areas.
elements.sent-2411-104: Rather than taking viewpoint variation as an obstacle for aligning satellite and drone views, we explicitly leverage the multi-view information and transform the drone-view video into Bird's Eye Views (BEVs).
elements.sent-2411-105: In doing so, we ease the learning process for the model.
elements.sent-2411-106: Instead of learning geometry correspondence and feature correspondences simultaneously, the model only needs to learn the feature mapping relationship between two views, thus significantly facilitating network convergence.
elements.sent-2411-107: Illustrated in the left of~\reffig{fig:architecture , given the drone-view video containing multi-view images, we estimate corresponding camera poses by structure from motion~\cite{ullman_sfm_1979 and reconstruct the scene containing the target buildings utilizing 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)~\cite{kerbl_3dgs_tg23 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: After reconstructing the scene, we adopt the normalized input pose and the unit vector in the world coordinate to calculate the BEV camera pose and render BEVs.
elements.sent-2411-109: For the training set, we incorporate rotation angles and varying heights into the BEV camera pose, generating a sequence of rotating and scaled-down BEVs.
elements.sent-2411-110: In terms of the test set, we only increase the height of the BEV camera poses and render a sequence of scaled-down BEV images.
elements.sent-2411-111: The number of BEVs corresponds to the number of images in the drone video.
elements.sent-2411-112: As shown in~\reffig{fig:architecture , outputs of Video2BEV transformation do not suffer from severe distortion, thereby aiding in the establishment of fine-grained spatial correspondence with the satellite view.}

Synthetic negatives exhibit similar colors and structures to original images, which assures the quality of negatives. } \label{fig:ddgs_visualization}
elements.sent-2411-113: Negative samples play a significant role in discriminative metric learning.
elements.sent-2411-114: Current negative sample mining strategies cannot ensure the quantity and quality of negative samples, as the number of challenging samples is limited, and selected negative samples can not always share similar architectural styles and other details with the original samples.
elements.sent-2411-115: In order to bypass these drawbacks, we propose to fine-tune a diffusion model to generate diverse samples as negative samples, shown in the right part of~\reffig{fig:architecture .}
elements.sent-2411-116: After transforming the drone-view video to BEVs $x_{bev}$ via the Video2BEV transformation, we utilize a visual-LLM~\cite{hu_minicpm_arxiv24 to generate captions in a multi-round process for both BEV and satellite-view images, during which a human annotator interacts with visual-LLM to restrict the token length of the captions.}
elements.sent-2411-117: After obtaining captions for the BEV and satellite images, we fine-tune a stable diffusion network~\cite{rombach_stable_diffusion_cvpr22 with LoRA~\cite{hu_lora_iclr22 to generate diverse synthetic images, during which we freeze the CLIP text encoder~\cite{radford_clip_icml21 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: The outputs of this model are challenging negative samples for the subsequent step.
elements.sent-2411-119: Considering the transformed BEV and satellite images share the same viewing direction and content, we conduct inference on the same stable diffusion network to generate negative samples for BEV and satellite-view images by adopting corresponding captions.
elements.sent-2411-120: We provide visualizations of original and synthetic images in~\reffig{fig:ddgs_visualization .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Similar to the original samples, synthetic negative samples exhibit comparable appearances, including color patterns and structural features of buildings.
elements.sent-2411-122: In this section, we do not overstate the novelty of the architecture.
elements.sent-2411-123: Instead, we adopt a general architecture from vision-language models~\cite{li_albef_nips21,li_blip_icml22 , enhanced by the synthetic negative samples.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The model architecture is shown in the middle of~\reffig{fig:architecture and is optimized in a two-stage manner.}
elements.sent-2411-125: In the first stage, we transform the drone-view video to BEVs by the proposed Video2BEV transformation (see the left part of \reffig{fig:architecture ).}
elements.sent-2411-126: Then, we adopt a shared encoder to extract embeddings from paired BEV and satellite-view images.
elements.sent-2411-127: The encoder is ViT-S~\cite{dosovitskiy_vit_iclr21 excluding the classifier.}
elements.sent-2411-128: The supervisions of this stage are the instance loss $\mathcal{L _I$ with the square-ring partition~\cite{wang_lpn_tcsvt21 and the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _C$~\cite{li_albef_nips21 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: For the instance loss, we first partition the embeddings from different views into different parts adopting the square-ring partition~\cite{wang_lpn_tcsvt21 strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Next, we apply multiple classifier modules to each part of the embeddings (similar to LPN~\cite{wang_lpn_tcsvt21 ), yielding {the location probability .}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Then we accumulate instance losses from multiple parts, and the instance loss $\mathcal{L _I$~\cite{zheng2017dual {is formulated as the location classification : \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_I= -log({\hat{p}_{sat}}) -log({\hat{p}_{bev}}),\end{equation} where $\hat{p _{sat}$ and $\hat{p _{bev}$ are the predicted probability that belongs to the ground-truth label from two views respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: For the contrastive loss, given a pair of satellite-view and BEV images, the satellite-to-BEV similarity is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:similarity_score} {S}_{sat2bev}=\frac{exp(s(f_{sat},f_{bev})/\tau)}{\Sigma^N_{j=1}exp(s(f_{sat}, f^j_{bev})/\tau)},\end{equation} where $f_{sat}$ and $f_{bev}$ are {the embeddings of the same location from two platforms, and $f^j_{bev}$ denotes the sample within the mini-batch.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Similarly, the BEV-to-satellite similarity is $S_{bev2sat}$ and the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _{C}$ is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{C} = -\frac{1}{2}(log(S_{sat2bev}) + log(S_{bev2sat})).\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-215: In this work, we propose to leverage videos to mitigate the impact of environmental constraints in drone visual geo-localization.
elements.sent-2411-216: We propose a new Video2BEV paradigm that transforms drone-view videos into Bird's Eye View (BEV) images by 3D gaussian splatting.
elements.sent-2411-217: This transformation effectively reduces the viewpoint gap between the drone view and the satellite view.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our Video2BEV paradigm also includes a diffusion-based module to generate negative samples, enhancing the scalability of the model.
elements.sent-2411-219: To validate the proposed method, we introduce the UniV dataset, a new video-based drone geo-localization dataset.
elements.sent-2411-220: The dataset includes flight paths of the drone at $30^\circ$ and $45^\circ$ elevation angles and corresponding videos recorded at up to 10 frames per second.
elements.sent-2411-221: Our Video2BEV paradigm outperforms other approaches in terms of Recall@1 and AP.

elements.sent-2411-471: Here, we propose a new research direction, namely, video-based geo-localization under rainy weather.
elements.sent-2411-472: Specifically, this setting aims to match drone-view videos (in rainy weather) with geo-tagged satellite images (in clean weather).
elements.sent-2411-474: We perform rainy data augmentation and out-of-distribution (OOD) testing on the $45^\circ$ \& 2 fps test set (see \reftab{tab:rainy_weather ).}
elements.sent-2411-475: Compared to Tab.~\textcolor{cvprblue {2a , all methods, including ours, show suboptimal performance, indicating that this setting is a challenging one.}}
elements.sent-2411-476: We explore two types of combinations of rainy data augmentation and the Video2BEV transformation in our method.
elements.sent-2411-477: The first type applies rainy data augmentation to the clean drone-view videos (Ours$^\dagger$ in \reftab{tab:rainy_weather ), meaning the augmentation is applied before the Video2BEV transformation.}
elements.sent-2411-478: The second type applies rainy data augmentation to the clean BEV videos (Ours$^*$ in \reftab{tab:rainy_weather ), meaning the augmentation is applied after the Video2BEV transformation.}
elements.sent-2411-479: The performance of these two combinations varies, which we attribute to the sensitivity of the vanilla version of 3DGS to the input in the Video2BEV transformation.
elements.sent-2411-480: Predicting accurate camera poses and point clouds from rainy videos remains challenging, leading to the imperfect visual quality of BEVs from rainy inputs (see \reffig{fig:rainy_weather}\textcolor{cvprblue {b ).}}
elements.sent-2411-482: However, we are confident that future advances in efficient 3DGS may help solve this problem.
